categories.title: The Rereadability Scale
categories.desc: Are you rereading this in five years or is it going straight in the library donation bin. 5 = actual reread, 1 = you'd rather reread a school pickup line group text.

categories.title: Best & Worst
categories.desc: Best character and worst character, back to back. “The protagonist” isn't an automatic answer for best, that's just the one who remembers everyone's birthday and never gets thanked. Worst is whoever should've been written out by chapter two and instead got a whole redemption arc nobody asked for.

categories.title: The Poor Man's ___
categories.desc: What book or author is this quietly copying. Extra credit for naming the exact book the author clearly had open in another tab.

categories.title: The “What Was She Thinking” Award
categories.desc: The single most eye-roll-inducing choice in the book. You cannot nominate yourself — somebody else has to back you up independently or it doesn't count.

categories.title: Casting Call
categories.desc: Real actors only. “I don't watch anything” is not a pass, guess anyway and defend it.

categories.title: Group Chat Test
categories.desc: Was there a line or scene worth screenshotting and sending unprompted. If nobody sent anything, ask why the book couldn't clear that bar.

categories.title: One Change
categories.desc: Rewrite one thing. Ending, character's fate, whatever. Defend it like the author is sitting across the table and can't leave.

categories.title: The Airport Test
categories.desc: Would you have actually bought this at the airport bookstore for a delay, or does it only survive because it was assigned.

categories.title: The Sunk Cost Award
categories.desc: For whoever kept reading a book they clearly weren't loving out of pure stubbornness, then finished it at midnight instead of admitting defeat.

categories.title: The Carpool Crew
categories.desc: One character you'd want in your carpool line when things go sideways, one you'd leave stranded without a second thought. Every book has both.

categories.title: The “I Read This Before Kids” Callback
categories.desc: Would you-before-motherhood have gotten anything from this, or does it only land now that you've got a mortgage, a carpool schedule, and none of the patience you used to fake having.

categories.title: The Almost Test
categories.desc: Is there a decision in this book you've personally almost made in real life. Confess or lie, either way it's a bit.

categories.title: The Sunday Scaries Rating
categories.desc: How wrecked did this leave you, same scale you already use for Sunday night dread instead of feelings. 5 = unprompted midnight text to the group chat, 1 = forgotten by carpool the next morning.

categories.title: Hot Take Insurance
categories.desc: Before discussion, everyone secretly predicts how the group reacts to the book. Wrong guesses owe a dollar to the class snack fund.

categories.title: Most Likely to Ruin a Toast
categories.desc: Which character, if they showed up tipsy to your wedding, gives the toast that ends the reception early.

categories.title: The Third Act Problem
categories.desc: Every movie falls apart in the third act. Does this book. Where exactly — “the ending” is not an answer.

categories.title: Oscar Bait or Actually Good
categories.desc: Is this book genuinely great or just dressed up to look important. Be honest about which one you fell for.

categories.title: The Studio Note
categories.desc: What would a nervous studio exec have demanded cut or changed to sell better, and would that have improved or ruined it.

categories.title: Rotten Tomatoes Split
categories.desc: Critics loved it, nobody actually enjoyed it, or the reverse. Where does this book land and why is the gap there.

categories.title: Sequel Nobody Asked For
categories.desc: Pitch the worst possible sequel, the one that exists purely to cash in. Bonus if it's more entertaining than the original.

categories.title: The Alibi
categories.desc: You didn't read it. Give the one detail you'd drop to convince the room you did. If it works, that's an indictment of the book.

categories.title: Devil's Advocate
categories.desc: Defend the antagonist like you're being paid by the hour. Build an actual case.

categories.title: True Crime Podcast Test
categories.desc: Could this plot be a two-part true crime podcast episode. Pitch the title and the ad read that comes right after the worst cliffhanger.

categories.title: No Filter
categories.desc: Two minutes, one person, straight roast of the author's choices. No compliments allowed, not even backhanded ones.

categories.title: Worst Seatmate
categories.desc: Which character would be the most insufferable person to get stuck next to on a four-hour flight with a crying toddler two rows back and no headphones.

categories.title: The One-Star Review
categories.desc: Write the pettiest possible one-star review this book absolutely has sitting on a retailer site somewhere.

categories.title: The Wikipedia Test
categories.desc: Be honest, would the plot summary have done the same job as the actual book.

categories.title: Would This Get Made Today
categories.desc: Could this get published or greenlit right now, or is it too much a product of when it came out.

categories.title: The Group Chat Favorites
categories.desc: Build the case for this book's spot in the group chat's all-time favorites, or admit it's just taking up space nobody will remember.

categories.title: The Book Swap
categories.desc: If you had to trade this book to a rival book club for one of their picks, what do you get back, and who got fleeced.

categories.title: The Hype Didn't Deliver Award
categories.desc: Every book has one: the character or plotline the marketing hyped hardest that never actually shows up once you're in it.

categories.title: The Quiet Standout
categories.desc: The minor character nobody expected anything from who quietly ends up carrying the whole book. Give her some flowers, nobody else will.

categories.title: The Foul Play Award
categories.desc: The single moment the author did something cheap enough to make you put the book down — a contrived twist, an unearned death, a cop-out ending.

categories.title: Did It Earn the Extra Pages
categories.desc: Does this book earn its extra length, or should it have wrapped up a hundred pages earlier.

categories.title: The Redemption Arc Award
categories.desc: A character written off early who completely turns it around by the end. If nobody qualifies, say that too.

categories.title: Did the Setting Do the Work
categories.desc: Did the setting do the heavy lifting here. Move the book somewhere else entirely and see if it survives the move.

categories.title: Best Group Chat Quote
categories.desc: Read your favorite line or put-down from the book like you're texting it to the group chat mid-read. Bonus if you can say it with a straight face.

categories.title: Who Didn't Make It
categories.desc: Which character got hurt, killed, or written out, and whether the book got better or worse the moment they left.

categories.title: The Plot Twist Nobody Called
categories.desc: The twist or turn nobody in this room saw coming. Group chat gasp or nothing.

categories.title: The Nightstand Test
categories.desc: Be honest about how this actually got read — cover to cover, audiobook at 1.5x during school drop-off, or crammed the last hundred pages the night before like a book report due tomorrow. No judgment. Immediate judgment.
